question.text: What distinguishes Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs) from conventional mutual funds?

answer.text: ETFs allow trading of units throughout the day on stock exchanges
ETFs are actively managed while mutual funds are passively managed
ETFs can only invest in equity instruments
ETFs do not have a Net Asset Value (NAV)

explanation.text: **The Logic:** ETFs are open-ended mutual funds that allow trading of their units throughout the day on exchanges. Conventional mutual funds allow buying and selling only at the closing NAV of the day. Additionally, ETFs are passively managed, while mutual funds are actively managed. **The Trap:** Option B reverses the fact — ETFs are passively managed, not actively. ETFs do have NAV and can invest in various asset classes. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** ETFs combine the diversification of mutual funds with the real-time trading flexibility of stocks. They track an index, so management costs are typically lower.
